data.title: Rocket Pharma Out Of Orbit, But Ready For Relaunch
data.description:
Rocket Pharmaceuticals (NASDAQ:RCKT) is a compelling gene therapy story with two differentiating factors. First, Rocket's therapies seemingly offer a life-long effect as they target cardiovascular and hematology, whereas many gene therapies (especially those targeting liver) display limited durability. Second, the target indications for Rocket's therapies are in diseases with limited or no treatment options with severe unmet needs. We are talking diseases so rare that the patient population for Kresladi is under 1000 in the US/EU. We see real long-term value in the company's AAV RP-A501 program targeting Danon Disease, a severe cardiovascular disease resulting from a type of lysosomal storage disorder that currently requires a heart transplant for real treatment, resulting in high rates of morbidity and mortality.

We see the near-term outlook to be driven by three upcoming clinical milestone expectations, most notably in RP-A601 and RP-A501, and the potential approval of Kresladi (if the company can get through its CRL). With the declining share price over the past year, and recent sell off, we see the current share price of $5 per share as an incredible value proposition for both short- and long-term investment opportunities. Rocket has $372M in cash and will be able to fund operations through the 1H of 2026, likely needing a financing later this year following either positive data readout or Kresladi approval as the company prepares for commercialization.

Excitingly, ahead of commercialization, the company announced in a filing that it has appointed Sarbani Chaudhuri as the new Chief Commercial and Medical Affairs Officer. Sarbani's extensive cell and gene therapy experience through her career, most recently as a VP at Johnson & Johnson, is a critical role as the company moves towards an approved product. Rocket is laying the framework for a successful launch, and this appointment really drives home the company's expectations of success.

We are very bullish and assign a buy rating to Rocket with a price target of $12 in 2025, with a more positive outlook long term.

Milestone expectations for the company in the coming months include:

  • RP-A501 - Program update in 1H 2025
  • RP-A601 - Phase I initial readout in 1H 2025
  • BAG3-DCM - IND Submission in 1H 2025
  • Kresladi Potential Approval in 2025

RP-A501 in Danon Disease

What is Danon disease? Danon disease is an X-linked lysosomal and glycogen storage disorder that results in severe cardiomyopathy. The tragedy of this disease is that the vast majority of patients don't live past 20, with male life expectancy to be approximately 20 years old while female life expectancy is approximately 35 due to later onset disease. There is no cure for the disease (fingers crossed RP-A501) and the only real treatment is a heart transplant, of which only about 20% of patients receive.

Clearly, there is a large unmet medical need for a treatment. RP-A501 is a single intravenous infusion of a gene therapy with a human lysosome-associated membrane protein 2 isoform B (LAMP2B) transgene load. Compared to a highly risky (transplant rejection, renal insufficiency, elevated infection, increased cancer risk) and probably the most invasive surgery one can have, a heart transplant, this has the potential to drastically change the treatment paradigm.

In the company's deck, they estimate that there are approximately 30,000 patients between the US and UK which is a small patient population. Although this may be underestimated, as management estimates only a 30% diagnosis rate. While a small patient population may seem daunting to investors and potential returns, let me highlight one key fact.

THERE ARE NO OTHER INVESTIGATIONAL DD TREATMENTS BEYOND RP-A501.

Now that we have established the need, let's get into the data.

RP-A501 is a AAV9 gene therapy with a LAMP2B load that has been evaluated in a Phase I study in seven male patients with pathogenic LAMP2 variant, cardiac involvement, and left ventricular ejection friction (LVEF) < 40%. The primary endpoints of the study surrounded safety, myocardial LAMP2 transduction and protein expression.

Treatment with RP-A501 was associated with cardiac LAMP2 expression in all evaluable patients at 12 months and demonstrated clinical improvements. Some of the clinical improvements include:

  • Reduction of left ventricular mass index (LVMI) of a median of 24%
    • Normal progression of disease is approximately 8% annual LVMI increase
  • Reduction or stabilization of cardiac biomarkers
    • NT-proBNP - 57% median reduction
    • Troponin I - 84% median reduction
  • Preservation of normalized left ventricular ejection friction
  • Large improvements in quality of life with all patients transitioning to Class I in NYHA class, leaving no clinical symptoms or limitations after treatment

One of the high dose cohort patients required steroid intervention, which may have altered the outcome for the LVMI. As a result, the company is progressing with a lower dosing schedule in the Phase II clinical study for an improved safety and efficacy profile.

The safety results from the study are also acceptable given the disease, with most adverse events occurring within the first 5 months and all serious adverse events within the first 4. Only 3 adverse events were reported beyond the first 12 months post infusion. Importantly, all serious adverse events were resolved without sequelae.

Completing Phase II enrollment in 3Q2024, the company is assessing RP-A501 in 12 patients 8 years and older at a dose lower than the high dose which had concerns from the Phase I study. Management has commented that it hopes to receive accelerated approval with endpoint data at 12 months as support, with full approval coming in tandem with longer follow up. With the current standing of the FDA, it is unknown if this kind of process where an additional trial past accelerated approval would be required, but it is best to lean to the safer side and expect the FDA to require the full trial schedule.

With the Phase II trial enrolling tow pediatric patients, a 3-month observational pre-treatment is required to establish a baseline in patients, which leads to a likely top-line data readout of the study in early 2026. We do expect an update in the coming months on the trial.

The Phase II trial being designed for an accelerated approval, and 12 month efficacy and safety data from the Phase I study really point to the high probability of approval. We expect data from the Phase II study to be slightly better than the Phase I with the singular dosing level that should eliminate concerns from the high dose cohort that were seen previously.

All this to say that an extremely high unmet need in a patient population that dies very young, coupled with no treatment options and promising efficacy and safety data, we are likely to see RP-A501 receive accelerated approval.

RP-A601 in PKP2-ACM

Rocket's other key in vivo asset that is in Phase I is RP-A601 a AAVrh74 based gene therapy for the treatment of Plakophilin 2 in arrhythmogenic cardiomyopathy. The asset has robust preclinical data that indicates great potential for Phase I and clinical development.

The company performed POC studies in mice models that demonstrated RP-A601 significantly reduced PKLP2 protein expression in cardiac tissue in tamoxifen-inducible mice leading to:

  • 56% reduction in LVEF
  • 95% increase in right ventricular area
  • 15% fibrosis in left ventricle
  • 38% fibrosis in right ventricle

There are concerns with translation to human studies as humans often carry single-allele mutations, these concerns are mitigated by the lack of evidence that PKP2 mutant proteins exert dominant negative functions and most mutations are truncating variants.

With the Phase I study well underway, we expect to see initial data in the coming months with a keen focus on the safety data as we also look at competitor updates from Trenaya and Lexeo who have similar Phase I programs in PKP2.

Kresladi

Almost a year ago, the FDA issued a CRL to Kresladi around stability and sterility that needed to be validated. We believe that this issue is minor and a BLA of Kresladi is likely to go through as Kresladi's profile is favorable, and the manufacturing process was fine, the CRL being unrelated to either processes.

Kresladi is a therapy designed for the treatment of leukocyte adhesion deficiency type I (LAD-I) that leads to patient death by age 2 in severe cases (75% of patients) and moderate cases patient death by 40 (>50% of patients). This disease prevents leukocytes from adhering to endothelial surfaces at the sites of inflammation because of a gene mutation encoding CD18. Ultimately, this leads to poor wound healing and constant infections that reduce the life span of patients. Kresladi is aiming to improve CD18 expression on leukocytes so that proper adhesion can occur.

Kresladi boasts a promising efficacy profile in its Pivotal Phase I/II trial, with 100% of patients (9/9) having CD18 expression on more than 10% polymorphonuclear leukocytes and 90% having expression on more than 40% at last follow up. This has lead to an approximate 70% reduction in incidence of infections in patients. Further, the company boasts a 100% survival rate for 20-42 month follow-ups in patients. This is important because the primary endpoint for the trials have been around survival exceeding 2 years post infusion, which Kresladi has exceeded.

The safety profile is also favorable, with no Kresladi related serious adverse events.

Risk

While we view the downside risk to Kresladi, RP-A501 and RP-A601 to be relatively low given the favorable safety and efficacy data in these rare disease markets with no treatments, we always have to be wary of additional safety signals. Any significant safety signal can delay or terminate the programs that have so far demonstrated outstanding safety profiles. Kresladi could also see a sub-par launch, if approved, which would be detrimental given the already miniscule market size.

RP-A601 is also facing clinical competition with Lexeo developing LX2020 and Tenaya developing TN-401, which are all targeting the same indication with the same cargo (different vehicles) and are all in Phase 1.

Rocket has also not announced the completion of dosing its program in RP-A501, which finished enrolling last year. This causes concern for the timeline of the program update.

The largest risk I see with the company currently sits at the FDA, which has seen a significant reduction in work force. Experts continue to debate on the impact these changes will have on regulatory approvals and trials, but it is all an unknown. We extend our views on timelines for trials and regulatory meetings with the FDA given the current constraints. Accelerated approval may be a little further out than expected.

Valuation

We value RP-A501 as the true driver behind Rocket's future. With Bloomberg estimates listing peak sales at around $2B, a high probably of success (80) on a 2-year launch timeline, and a 15% discount rate, we get an NPV for RP-A501 of around $8.5B. Turning to the combined secondary programs, we see estimates of peak sales at approximately $1B per year, discounted the same way on a 3-year launch timeline and a much lower overall probability of success of 40%, we get an NPV of approximately $2B. In total, we value the pipeline NPV at $10.5B.

Adjusting the discount to NPV due to pre-approval stage down 80%, we get a valuation of $2.1B and a share price of $21. While optimistic on this price point, we recognize the current market environment and the current price of $5. Our near term price target is roughly half that value at $12 per share.

Conclusion

We believe that Rocket Pharmaceuticals is positioned well for a hard rebound going into the second half of 2025. With the recent market action driving the share price to insane discounted valuations, we see a massive opportunity to pick up an undervalued stock. We have numerous updates for the pipeline coming in the next few months, most notably an update on RP-A501 that will relieve the investor pressure on the stock.

Milestone expectations for the company in the coming months to drive shareholder value include:

  • RP-A501 - Program update in 1H 2025
  • RP-A601 - Phase I initial readout in 1H 2025
  • BAG3-DCM - IND Submission in 1H 2025
  • Kresladi Potential Approval in 2025

The company's programs represent a small addressable market that is severely underserved, while its therapeutics appear to be clinically effective with impressive safety profiles. We expect these safety data to continue forward through readouts.

We still find it hard to believe that the stock is down 50%+ just year to date, compared to the XBI at approximately down 20%.

We are very bullish and assign a strong buy rating to Rocket with a price target of $12 in 2025, with a more positive outlook long term.

data.title: From OPEC To Cleantech: Key Drivers Of EM Commodities
data.description:
Unique forces are set to shape the commodity markets in 2025: plentiful supplies threaten oil; rising tariff risks threaten the cleantech momentum for copper; supply challenges boost aluminium; and gold shines brighter amid economic and geopolitical uncertainty.

This commentary is based on a March 4, 2025, webinar.

Crude Oil

Global crude oil consumption was approximately 103 million barrels per day as of year-end 2024, with emerging markets (EMs) the main drivers of consumption. By 2030, we expect crude oil consumption to grow to about 105 million barrels, with EMs continuing to drive consumption and developed market countries reducing their usage. Gasoline remains the primary driver of oil demand, but its growth is expected to slow with rising electric vehicle (EV) adoption and the energy transition. However, this decline should be offset by increasing demand for industrial and jet fuels (which aren’t significantly cleaner).

On the supply side, OPEC’s share of global crude production has fallen from nearly 36% to below 30% as of 2024.

The United States has largely filled this gap, with shale production expanding significantly. The new administration aims to increase U.S. output from 13 million to 16 million barrels per day over time. Achieving this may be challenging, but a key distinction of U.S. shale production is its short lead time. Unlike traditional oil projects, which take three to five years from investment decision to production, U.S. shale can ramp up within a year (and sometimes within three months), making it a much faster and more flexible supply source.

Looking at other parts of the world, Asian oil production has declined slightly, while Europe’s, though not a major contributor, has dropped significantly. Russia has faced constraints, though less severe than initially expected under sanctions. However, some regions are seeing growth.

One of the fastest-growing producers is Guyana, where output has surged from near zero just a few years ago to around 650,000 barrels per day. Estimates suggest it could reach 1.5 million to 2 million barrels per day, making it one of the largest sources of new supply.

The outlook for other suppliers is mixed.

Mexico’s Pemex faces ongoing challenges, with production falling from 2.5 million to 1.6 million barrels per day due to underinvestment, brain drain, and aging infrastructure. Without significant reforms or partnerships—unlikely but possible—this decline will be difficult to reverse.

Brazil, on the other hand, is a growing focus. Petrobras has expanded production to nearly 3 million barrels per day while divesting assets, fueling the rise of smaller exploration and production (E&P) companies and service providers. This ecosystem is creating new investment opportunities, with production growth and sector maturity expected to continue.

Canada remains a key producer, though U.S.-Canada tariff tensions add some uncertainty. However, its heavier crude requires specialized refining, particularly in the U.S. Midwest, creating a symbiotic relationship that is likely to endure despite political volatility.

But it’s not just about supply and demand; as a quote from the International Energy Agency (IEA; circa 2005-2006) states, “Oil is driven by a multitude of factors that vary in influence over time.” Our approach involves analyzing these many variables, so let’s look at a few.

We believe several positive factors are likely to support oil markets.

  • U.S. demand is expected to rise slightly, aided by a favorable energy policy environment.
  • China, despite recent slowing, should see a rebound, albeit at a more moderate growth rate.
  • Non- Organization for Economic Cooperation and Development demand remains a strong driver as global growth outside developed markets continues.
  • U.S. pressure on rogue producers such as Iran and Venezuela could tighten supply, creating further upward momentum.
  • Lastly, global inventories are currently low, adding another layer of support for oil prices.

Additional positive factors, though less certain, could also support oil markets.

  • OPEC has been holding back production since COVID, and while some barrels have been released, a full return remains uncertain.
  • If supply from rogue producers is curtailed, OPEC may be incentivized to release more barrels selectively.
  • Low speculative positioning is another potential driver—financial market activity in crude futures could create volatility, but if positioning remains low, it may support prices as it normalizes.
  • Geopolitical risks, while unpredictable, tend to drive an oil price premium when tensions rise.
  • OPEC’s supply constraints have increased spare capacity, providing a potential buffer for market stability.

We believe several negative factors are likely to impact oil markets.

  • The oil-dollar correlation fluctuates, but a strong dollar generally weighs on oil prices, as we’ve seen recently.
  • U.S. supply growth, coupled with headlines about unleashing U.S. energy dominance, could also put downward pressure on prices.
  • Non-OPEC supply growth—from producers like Guyana, Brazil, the United States, and Canada—adds to the supply-side pressure.
  • Additionally, rising EV penetration continues to erode gasoline demand, creating a long-term headwind for oil consumption.

Additional negative factors, though less certain, could also put downward pressure on oil markets.

  • At some point, OPEC will release withheld barrels, increasing supply and weighing on prices.
  • Capital expenditure (CAPEX) budgets are also rising, particularly among supermajors, even as companies continue to generate free cash flow.
  • Lastly, the energy transition remains a long-term factor. While U.S. policy shifts may slow its pace, the global push toward alternative energy sources continues to reshape demand dynamics.

After considering these factors, what’s in store for oil prices? Looking at historical trends provides some context. During COVID we saw WTI prices briefly dip into negative territory due to extraordinary circumstances, while Brent remained low but never turned negative. As demand rebounded and COVID-related shutdowns eased, prices surged past $100, further driven by the Russia-Ukraine war and resulting sanctions on a major oil producer. OPEC’s gradual release of withheld barrels then put downward pressure on prices, leading to a stabilization between $70 and $90 over the past few years. Despite fluctuations, oil prices have remained relatively within this range.

Looking ahead, we expect some U.S. demand growth and a boost from China’s stimulus, both positive factors. On the other hand, OPEC’s eventual supply release and rising non-OPEC production will likely exert downward pressure on prices. Currently, Brent sits around $75 per barrel and WTI around $71. Most sell-side analysts anticipate further declines, and we share that view.

For the companies we invest in, the focus remains on cost structure, operational efficiency, and barrel replacement rather than short-term oil price fluctuations. Management teams recognize that crude price volatility is inevitable, so we prioritize companies that can operate sustainably at $70 per barrel without significant credit risks. If prices rise, credit metrics improve but most of the upside accrues to equity value.

Copper

Copper is an industrial metal with broad applications. It’s used by a variety of industries, including construction (28%) and power (16%), the latter of which is increasing its share due to renewable energy applications. Use in transportation (13%) is also expanding, driven by EVs, which use three to four times more copper than conventional cars.

The energy transition is a major driver of copper demand, with clean energy currently accounting for about 25% of consumption. This share is projected to rise to 45% by 2040, with overall demand expected to increase by 40%—an additional 11 million tons—according to the IEA.

China accounts for nearly 60% of global copper demand, though its growth has fluctuated. In 2024, refined copper demand in China grew just 2%, compared to 9% in 2023 and 25% in 2021, largely driven by grid and renewable energy investments and EVs.

On the supply side, more than 80% of global mine production comes from EMs. Chile leads with 24% of global output, while the Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC) has seen double-digit production growth in recent years. Peru remains a major supplier as well.

After extraction, copper must be smelted and refined, with China dominating global refining capacity at 45%.

Copper prices surged in 2024, particularly in May, driven by short squeezes on Comex and speculation about a broader metals supercycle. Prices are also highly sensitive to China’s economic headlines, particularly around stimulus measures. In 2020 and 2021, supply concerns and low inventories pushed prices higher and kept them elevated.

Looking ahead, trade wars present the biggest downside risk, with U.S.-China tariffs already announced and further escalation a key uncertainty. However, strong green energy demand remains a positive catalyst, alongside China’s stimulus measures, which have yet to fully impact the market.

Gold

Gold demand saw strong positive trends last year, prompting a greater focus on the metal. The three main demand drivers are jewelry, which accounts for 50% of global demand, followed by central bank purchases and investment demand—including futures, exchange-traded funds (ETFs), coins, and bars.

China and India together make up about half of global gold demand, with historically strong physical consumption. In 2024, physical demand increased, though China’s numbers were more muted, possibly reflecting differing economic sentiment between the two countries.

Since 2022, central bank gold purchases have remained high, contributing significantly to demand. In 2024 we saw gold purchases at similar levels to 2022, which was a year of high demand of gold from central banks. China, in particular, has been steadily increasing its gold reserves over the past 15 years, accelerating since 2022 as part of a broader de-dollarization strategy and a hedge against geopolitical risks. Other major holders include Japan, Switzerland, and the United States.

Investor positioning has also been strengthening, with futures markets showing increasing long positions—a sign of optimism. Similar trends are evident in ETFs, bars, and coins, though positioning does not yet appear overstretched, suggesting further upside potential.

Gold prices recently broke above $2,950, continuing a strong upward trend throughout 2024. Several factors have contributed, including geopolitical risks and economic uncertainty—drivers that are expected to persist, which may limit downside risks. However, sustained high prices could dampen demand, particularly in the jewelry sector and potentially among central banks.

Aluminum

Aluminum, another industrial metal, shares many similarities with copper, and has comparable demand trends across key sectors.

China dominates the market, accounting for 58% of global aluminum demand and holding the largest smelting capacity, making it the top producer. Europe also plays a significant role, representing 14% of demand.

The energy transition is a major driver, with green demand—renewable energy, EVs, and grid updates—currently making up 8% of total aluminum demand. This share is expected to rise to 18% by 2030, further supporting long-term growth.

In 2024, aluminum prices were elevated due to supply disruptions in alumina, a key feedstock, which squeezed producer margins and provided cost support for prices. Supply-driven price surges also occurred in 2022 following Russia’s invasion of Ukraine, as concerns over disruptions temporarily pushed aluminum prices higher. While supply chains have since adjusted, we believe ongoing energy transition demand and potential supply constraints create a positive outlook for aluminum going forward.

The Investment Opportunities

We’ve analyzed the fundamentals of key commodity sectors within the EM debt universe, including independent and national oil, aluminum, copper, and gold companies.

Leverage trends have generally improved since the COVID period, with a notable decline in aluminum companies. Gold companies, in contrast, have maintained stable leverage, reflecting their strong credit quality—something also seen in other sectors.

Margins across most commodity sectors remain high, at or above 50%, and have been relatively stable over time. Aluminum, however, continues to experience lower margins due to its energy-intensive and higher-cost production process, making it a structurally less profitable sector compared to others.

But understanding a company’s position in its credit cycle is crucial to making sense of bond yields. Most commodity sectors offer a broad range of yields, creating diverse investment opportunities. Gold and copper companies, for example, typical yield between 5% and 8% in today’s markets. Aluminum yields tend to be a bit lower. Independent oil companies vary significantly, with smaller producers (20,000 to 30,000 barrels per day) differing from larger ones (150,000 to 250,000 barrels per day), but yields generally average close to 9%.

On the national oil company side, yields range from 5% to 9%, influenced by credit quality and country risk, with an average around 7%. Duration is less of a factor in the difference in yields, as most bonds in this universe are five- to seven-year maturities, though national oil companies sometimes issue longer-dated debt. Overall, there is a wide set of opportunities for investors to identify the best risk-reward balance.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Vail Resorts: 6% Yield With Global Ski Empire Moving In The Right Direction
data.description:
Vail Resorts (NYSE:MTN) has been a lightning rod for criticism lately. From its core skier base, customers are complaining about hefty price increases and crowded lift lines. On the other side, investors have complained about sluggish revenue growth and poor stock market returns, as shares of Vail have sunk ~40% over the past year.

Yet at the same time, Vail looks a lot better in 2025 than it did in 2024, a year that was beset by warm weather conditions and fewer open days for many of Vail’s resorts. Amid the latest fresh drop in Vail stock, it’s a good time for investors to re-assess the bull case in this name.

I last wrote a buy opinion on Vail in December, when the stock was still trading at $195 per share. I praised the company’s compelling valuation amid its transformative restructuring plan, which alongside cutting corporate headcount also aimed at pooling global customer service resources and modernizing many of its resorts. Since then, Vail has continued to drop, while the company released (in my view) healthy FY25 results driven by price increases. As such, I’m reiterating my buy opinion on Vail.

Here is what fuels my enthusiasm for Vail: in my view, the company still has plenty of room to boost revenue through price increases. Management just kicked off Epic Pass sales for the 2025/2026 ski season, and it noted that pass prices are up ~7% on average versus this year.

Skiing, historically, has been a high-end and expensive sport. COVID coincided with the exploding popularity of the Epic Pass that revolutionized the ski industry: suddenly, many cooped-up people seeking outdoor recreation found themselves on the slopes for the first time, leading to the crowded lift lines that Vail is still receiving complaints about. But season to date, the company notes that total skier visitation is down -2.5% versus the same period last year.

We don’t know whether that’s a reflection of one-time skiers from the COVID era dropping the habit, a reaction to price increases, or both - but either way, the company is enjoying net revenue growth from the price increases despite visitation drops, indicating that its core customer base is relatively inelastic. And in my view, the company can justify its price increases in part due to the vast upgrades that the company is making across its resorts, particularly in upgrading high-speed ski lifts. Management notes so far that in the 2024/2025 ski season, wait times exceeding 10 minutes happened only 3% of the time.

In other words, after dealing with a sharp burst of overcrowding, I believe the ski business is finding supply-demand balance again, at a higher price point that benefits Vail. It may take time for the company to regain positive feedback from its customer base, but in the meantime we like the decent revenue growth from both price increases plus growth in ancillary services like dining, equipment rentals, and ski schools. We should note as well that despite many headlines claiming the company is in trouble, Vail still maintained its FY25 outlook as well as its $2.22 quarterly dividend, which represents a handsome ~5.5% yield at today’s share prices - a decent incentive to keep investors patient while Vail completes its overhaul.

It doesn't hurt either that Vail's impacts from recent tariff war escalations should be minimal (only second-order impacts from macroeconomic shocks potentially reducing customers' willingness to spend on ski trips). Stay long here and use the dip in Vail as a buying opportunity.

Q2 recap

Let’s now go through Vail’s latest quarterly results in greater detail. The fiscal Q2 (January quarter) results are shown below:

Vail's revenue grew 5.5% y/y to $1.14 billion, accelerating three points versus 2.3% y/y growth in the preceding Q1. The growth was largely driven by a 6.9% y/y jump in lift tickets, which in turn was driven by the price increases made to the Epic Pass for the 2024/2025 season (the company recognizes Epic Pass revenue ratably throughout the ski season, even though it sells the tickets well in advance). Lift ticket revenue represents roughly two-thirds of Vail's total. Also, we note that ski school revenue of $133.0 million grew 5% y/y, while dining increased 11% y/y to $90.9 million.

A couple of visitation trends are worth noting. First, while season-to-date visitation is down -2.5% as mentioned previously, the mix of visitors has tilted more toward local skiers, which in turn has boosted ski school and rentals revenue. Looking out at the remainder of the season, however, the company is expecting a bigger influx of "destination" visitors, based on its current guest reservations and lodging bookings - which should help the company's lodging segment in Q3.

The company notes that weather conditions are largely normal, when looking at a blend across the company's 42 resorts - which is a relief after an unusually warm winter in 2023. Management notes that visitation accelerated in Q2 - but the company did not that visits trailed off in February as expected, something we'll have to watch out for on Vail's Q3 print. Per CEO Kristen Lynch's remarks on the Q&A portion of the Q2 earnings call on visitation metrics:

Regarding Q2 versus the season-to-date metrics, which included February, so Q2 our visitation at our North American resorts was slightly above prior year levels. So benefited from the improved conditions, offset a little bit by -- as we talked about at the beginning of our fiscal year, the continued industry demand normalization and some shift that we're seeing in guest behavior into the spring vacation timing for destination guests.

So Q2 visitation was above prior year. When you include February, we did see February visitation contracted versus prior year, which we expected. December and January were easier year-over-year comparisons given the significant conditions impact that we had last year. So February was a little bit more challenging. And of course, we do see that industry demand normalization."

On the profitability front, Vail's total adjusted EBITDA grew 8.2% y/y to $458.1 million:

Margins in the mountain segment improved thanks to price increases in the Epic Pass and better ancillary sales, such as dining. However, the lodging segment sliced down to near-zero profitability, as RevPAR declined -2.5% y/y to $155.23 (RevPAR or revenue per available room is one of the most commonly watched metrics in the hotel industry, calculated as occupancy rates multiplied by average daily rates). The company did note, however that it expects "destination" visitors to pick up later in the ski season, based on the company's own hotel bookings - so we should see the lodging segment pick up again.

Risks and key takeaways

Of course, there are risks that we need to keep in mind for Vail. The company's 2025/2026 Epic Pass sales kickoff, and its associated ~7% price increase, is also coinciding with a volatile market correction in calendar Q1. The "wealth effect" triggered by this market backlash could make many of Vail's affluent buyers push back more against these price increases.

At the same tine, however, I believe these risks are well captured in Vail's modest valuation multiples: as shown above, the stock trades at <10x forward adjusted EBITDA. On top of Vail's recommitment to its $2.22 quarterly dividend (which yields ~6.3% at current share prices), investors have plenty of reasons to be patient here.

data.title: Ironwood Pharmaceuticals: Setting A Strong Foundation For A Return To Growth
data.description:
I’ve always been intrigued by Ironwood Pharmaceuticals (NASDAQ:IRWD), dating back about seven years ago when I kept a close eye on the company as I was trying to figure out if Synergy Pharmaceuticals could eventually get enough traction to make Trulance a legitimate threat to LINZESS. At that time, I was deeply impressed by Ironwood’s strategy to work with AbbVie to develop and co-market LINZESS. After stepping back for a while, I decided to take a look at their Q4 and full-year 2024 results, and it was clear the company is dealing with a difficult transition with LINZESS, but it is nearly on the doorstep of a major win with their apraglutide prospect for short bowel syndrome (SBS). The company’s significant advances in both their clinical pipeline and financial performance have set a foundation for substantial growth potential in the coming years. As a result, I find myself rediscovering IRWD as an attractive investment, and I will be on the lookout for an opportunity to initiate a position

I intend to provide some background information on Ironwood Pharmaceuticals and the company’s recent performance. In addition, I will highlight some of the company’s efforts to return to growth. Then, I will take a look at the company’s valuation versus its growth prospects. In addition, I will point out some of the downside risks that investors should consider when managing their position. Finally, I take a look at the Daily Chart to see if I can identify an entry point.

Background on Ironwood

Ironwood Pharmaceuticals is committed to developing therapies for gastrointestinal (GI) conditions and diseases. Ironwood’s flagship product is LINZESS (linaclotide), the leading U.S.-branded prescription for adults with Irritable Bowel Syndrome with Constipation and Chronic Idiopathic Constipation (IBS-C and CIC), as well as for pediatric patients aged 6 to 17 with functional constipation. Ironwood has had a longstanding partnership with AbbVie (ABBV) to co-develop and co-commercialize LINZESS in the U.S., which has led to the label being expanded, including an FDA approval for pediatric functional constipation.

The company’s lead pipeline program is apraglutide, a long-acting synthetic GLP-2 analog for short bowel syndrome (SBS) who depend on parenteral support (PS). Ironwood acquired apraglutide with their $1B acquisition of VectivBio in 2023. Apraglutide is moving closer to the finish line in SBS, and the company has initiated an NDA submission with the FDA, which is expected to be complete by Q3 of this year. If approved, apraglutide will be the first and only once-weekly GLP-2 therapy for SBS patients. The latest data from the open-label extension “STARS Extend” study showcased apraglutide’s ability to produce enteral autonomy, so the approval outlook is promising.

Ironwood is also expanding their pipeline beyond GI disorders with IW-3300, their wholly owned GC-C agonist for visceral pain conditions such as interstitial cystitis/bladder pain syndrome (IC/BPS) and endometriosis. Both of IW-3300’s target conditions are largely markets with limited treatment options that collectively impact millions of people in the United States. Ironwood is now progressing IW-3300 through a proof-of-concept study in IC/BPS.

Recent Performance Review

Ironwood recently reported their Q4 and full-year 2024 earnings, with their total Q4 revenue coming in at $90.5M, which was down from $117.6M in Q4 of 2023. For the full year 2024, total revenue came in at $351.4M, but this was a decrease from $442.7M they reported for 2023. Despite the drop in revenue, the company still reached a GAAP net income of $2.3M in Q4, compared to the $1.1M net loss recorded in Q4 of 2023. Ironwood's full-year GAAP net income was $0.91M, a massive improvement over the $1B net loss in 2023.

Looking a bit deeper, LINZESS reported $88.4M in Q4 for collaboration revenue, which was down 22% from the $114M the company reported in Q4 of 2023. It turns out the U.S. net sales of LINZESS dropped by 15% year-over-year despite prescription demand growth.

Even after 12 years of being on the market, LINZESS is still the leader with demand continuing to grow. The company saw a 12% bump in prescription demand growth versus Q4 of 2023, and an 11% increase in prescription demand for full-year 2024 compared to 2023. Ironwood also noted that their new-to-brand volume growth was 11% for Q4 and 14% for the full year. Interestingly, Q4’s numbers are the eighth consecutive quarter for double-digit new-to-brand volume growth.

Although pricing headwinds from Medicaid utilization rates continue to hurt the earnings, the commercial numbers for LINZESS are only strengthening. That being said, the earnings are becoming a concern. The company has struggled to hit the Street’s expectations for at least a few years now.

Although the company has been able to report a positive EPS, it is fair to say the erratic earnings are troublesome for investors.

Moreover, the company has performed some restructuring early this year in order to streamline their operations and focus on advancing apraglutide. Ironwood reduced their workforce by around 50%, which is projected to save them $55M to $60M annually, and benefit their bottom line $40M to $45M.

Apraglutide To The Rescue?

As I mentioned above, Ironwood initiated a rolling NDA submission to the FDA for apraglutide in Short Bowel Syndrome with Intestinal Failure (SBS-IF) patients with colon-in-continuity (CIC), with completion expected by Q3 of this year. The latest data from the STARS Extend study shows the drug’s prospects to reduce the dependence on PS, which is a critical measure for patients with SBS. The data showed that 78% of patients had gained at least one day off PS at 52 weeks, with the mean PS volume being reduced by 52%. The company also reported that these reductions in the need for PS were observed as early as four weeks, with these effects being sustained for the length of the study.

Compared to PN and other GLP-2 analogs like GATTEX (teduglutide) from Takeda (TAK), apraglutide offers several advantages. GATTEX is currently the only approved GLP-2 analog for SBS-IF, has been shown to reduce PS, but only in a subset of patients. In addition, GATTEX’s effect appears to be more gradual and might not deliver the same rapid and sustained improvements seen in apraglutide’s STARS Extend study. In addition, apraglutide’s long-acting formulation allows for once-weekly sub-q injections, which should improve patient adherence and quality of life versus GATTEX’s daily injections.

Although approval is not guaranteed, I believe apraglutide’s combination of efficacy in reducing PS dependency, rapid onset of action, and sustained benefits presents a strong case to the FDA. Not only does Ironwood have a promising candidate, but the FDA should also consider the strength of the drug’s profile over other treatment options, including keeping patients dependent on PS, which has associated risks, including infections and malnutrition.

If approved, Ironwood would be entering a U.S. SBS market that was valued at $553.3M in 2024, and has a global market that could hit $5.4B by 2030. The SBS-IF market is projected to be about 18K patients in the U.S., Europe, and Japan, so it is not a huge market to cover. Therefore, a new therapeutic could quickly claim a good percentage of these markets. GATTEX pulled in $806M in 2023 and $226M in Q1 of fiscal 2024, and it has U.S. peak sales estimate is $350M, so I think it is appropriate to believe that apraglutide could achieve a similar mark in its commercial lifetime.

Assuming apraglutide has pricing parity with GATTEX of $300K-$400K ($350K for calculations) a year, and Ironwood gets 3% penetration of the roughly 10K SBS patients in the United States, we could see nearly $105M in revenue in 2026. If that is moved up to 7.5%, we are looking at $262.5M in 2027, and possibly $787.5M by 2030. What is more, the $787.5M by 2030 in U.S. sales in a DCF model with 85% gross and 55% operating margins could give Ironwood free cash flow ramps from $58M in 2026 to $433M in 2030, with a terminal value of ~$5.5B based on $385M in 2035 FCF. Discounting all cash flows gives us a ~$3.9B NPV.

Keep in mind, I am being conservative and only considering the U.S. market. Meanwhile, the company believes apraglutide’s peak sales can hit $1B, and some analysts are projecting $1.3B. Indeed, these figures are leaning on projections along with strong pricing power, favorable reimbursement, and exclusivity into 2036. However, it does illustrate how the U.S. SBS market could be very lucrative, and possibly get Ironwood back on a growth trajectory.

Looking For A Turnaround

As I look deeper into 2025, I get that cliché stance of being cautiously optimistic about Ironwood’s potential for a turnaround in the coming years. I think it is fair to say that apraglutide is a promising asset that has the potential to become a prominent treatment for some SBS patients. The rolling NDA submission could put apraglutide’s PDUFA date in the first half of 2026, with a potential launch in the same calendar year. Adding another commercial product will provide some additional revenue and should improve the company’s long-term outlook.

Indeed, adding another commercial product should raise some expenses, however, the company’s cost-cutting measures should allow them to focus on their core assets while still keeping the finances at a manageable level. I must also point out that despite the declining revenue from LINZESS, the company still expects to pull in roughly $260M-$290M in 2025, with more than $85M in Adjusted EBITDA.

In fact, Ironwood's CEO, Tom McCourt, stated,

We expect the cash flows from LINZESS will continue to sustain future capital needs to fund the next stage of growth for Ironwood, namely the commercial approval and launch of apraglutide for short bowel syndrome patients who are dependent on parental support.

So, as long as there is continued demand for LINZESS, and apraglutide is on track for approval in 2026, we could see the market start to see a change in its stance on IRWD as the ingredients for growth begin to assemble. At the moment, it looks as if the Street is expecting similar revenue for Ironwood as the company’s guidance. However, I will point out that some analysts are expecting the company to have a strong 2026, with revenue hitting roughly $317M. Although the Street’s revenue estimates for Ironwood appear to be all over the map, the general trend is up heading into the next decade.

As for EPS, The Street is expecting a more orderly growth trajectory with strong double-digit and triple-digit growth heading over the next 10 years, going from about 0.17 in 2025 to around 1.46 in 2033.

Indeed, these are just estimates, but one can see how other analysts are expecting Ironwood to make the transition from a LINZESS warden to GI specialist with an intriguing growth narrative that can be funded by their legacy product. Assuming Ironwood is able to hit $343M in revenue in 2030, which would be a 0.56x forward price-to-sales. The industry’s average price-to-sales is 4x-5x, so $343M would justify roughly $8.50 to $10.50 per share. Considering the ticker is trading just above $1.00 per share, I think it is appropriate to say that IRWD is a prime turnaround candidate.

Risks To Consider

Although Ironwood appears to be offering a great opportunity for speculative investors, we have to acknowledge that the company still has plenty of risks. First, the turnaround storyline is centered on apraglutide getting approved in SBS-IF with CIC. As I mentioned above, apraglutide did have positive results for its Phase III trial. However, following the readout, IRWS dropped nearly 40%, which appears to be mostly attributed to the two missed secondary endpoints in CIC patients. It looks as if apraglutide struggled to show an ability to significantly reduce parenteral support days or achieve enteral autonomy at 48 weeks in this subgroup. Yes, this is concerning because that would be the subset of SBS patients the company is attempting to target with apraglutide. Ironwood attributed the outcome to a high placebo response influenced by trial design, so apraglutide wasn’t able to show a huge benefit over placebo in the endpoints. Overall, the data does appear to be strong, but it is fair to question if the FDA is going to see it as an upgrade over GATTEX and give them the green light for the label they are targeting.

Another concern is whether providers are going to see apraglutide as the superior choice over GATTEX. At the moment, GATTEX’s indications are adults and pediatric patients 1 year and older with SBS.

That label should allow GATTEX to be a reliable option for all SBS patients, and could prevent Ironwood from capturing a broader market share. The other big concern is generics… not for apraglutide, but GATTEX. It looks as if GATTEX generics should have the green light come November of this year, which is before apraglutide could be approved. At the moment, I can’t find any evidence of a generic being tested for the U.S., but it looks as if Adalvo is targeting a GATTEX biosimilar in Europe. Of course, other candidates are being developed for SBS that could challenge Ironwood and Takeda, including Jaguar Health (JAGX) who is attempting to get an SBS added to Crofelemer’s label. Zealand Pharma (OTCPK:ZLDPF) is aiming for SBS with glepaglutide. The FDA rejected glepaglutide in December of 2024, and the company appears to be ready to run a Phase III trial to gather more data for the FDA. In addition, we could see some gut-trophic hormones, including GLP-2 variants, and GLP-2 analogs that could be developed in the coming years.

The company also has to consider strong competition for LINZESS, including TRULANCE, which is now being handled by Bausch Health Companies (BHC). The pricing pressure and strong competition from TRULANCE may erode revenues faster than Ironwood is anticipating.

Another overlooked risk is IRWD's shares are highly illiquid, with only ~1,000 shares traded daily. A low liquidity trading environment for a speculative ticker results in big bid-ask spreads, price volatility, and possibly difficulty entering and exiting positions under certain conditions. So, investors need to be aware of these aspects and how they can impact thier P&L. So, maybe IRWD is not a prime target for investors who are unfamiliar with trading thinly traded stocks.

Last but not least, investors need to keep an eye on the company’s financials. Yes, the company is reporting a positive EPS, and company management believes LINZESS should provide enough cash to fund their next stage of growth. However, the company's cash position was roughly $88.56M at the end of 2024, and they have ~$599.48M in debt.

The acquisition of VectivBio brought on the revolving credit facility and impairment charges, so the company could face financial strain going forward… especially if the apraglutide fails to make it to the market. The small cash position could be a lingering concern until the company completes a strong turnaround.

Considering these risks, I am assigning IRWD a conviction level of 2 out 5, and the ticker will be considered for the Compounding Healthcare “Bio Boom” Portfolio.

Looking For A Buy

I am going to keep an eye on IRWD to start a speculative position, but I'll first focus on technical indicators to find a good entry point. Typically, I look for several key signals to point to a potential reversal before dipping my toe, such as a strong bounce on key support levels, climbing above moving averages, or a bullish divergence in the RSI. Looking at IRWD’s Daily Chart, I don’t see an abundance of bullish signals.

The price action screams bearish; however, I will point out that a bullish divergence in RSI is forming, and the trading volume is increasing as the share price approaches $1 per share. Although both points are not bullish, one big day to the upside could quickly change IRWD’s technical ratings. Therefore, I am going to wait for a break above the downtrend ray from the February 2024 high before considering a buy order. Then, I will wait for a break above the anchored VWAPs from the December 2024 high, before making another addition. Once that has transpired, I will wait for Ironwood to complete their NDA submission for apraglutide, and wait for a dip following the announcement or any pullback to add more shares. After that, I’ll set some sell orders in order to book some profits and move the IRWD position to a “House Money” status to hold for a long-term investment funded by the market.

For the long term, my strategy will be to continue trading IRWD over the next five years in anticipation of the company receiving FDA approval, launching apraglutide, and returning to growth.

data.title: IAMGOLD Corporation: Sparkling, But Not My Nugget Of Choice
data.description:
IAMGOLD Corporation (NYSE:IAG) has experienced a stellar performance over the last year, but with many gold mining stocks to choose from, it is not my preferred pick in the sector. I will explain why I favour Harmony Gold (NYSE:HMY) and Pan African Resources (OTCQX:PAFRY) at the moment, in particular.

Gold Above $3,000

With nerves fraying, investors have sought comfort in gold's timeless embrace. I must admit that it has surprised me somewhat to see the gold price pull back a bit after President Trump's announcement of "Liberation Day" tariffs (although gold is still trading comfortably above the $3,000 mark) - but I attributed a portion of this move to panic selling, and investors liquidating positions to meet margin calls, as well as a likely rotation into alternative assets. Notably, markets were off to the races on the 9th of April when President Trump announced that there would be a 90-day pause on reciprocal tariffs for countries that did not retaliate against the U.S. - but I still hold the view that we will remain in a period of heightened geopolitical tension, which tends to support the gold price.

Central Banks have continued buying gold in large quantities. The World Gold Council reported that official gold reserves of Central Banks around the world increased by another 24 tonnes in February, alone. It is my view that in the wake of President Trump's "Liberation Day" tariffs, many central banks around the world, especially those that are hostile towards the United States, like some members in the BRICS bloc are, will continue selling Dollar assets (including US treasuries), and replace a portion of those assets with gold . Notably, China is the second-largest foreign holder of US treasuries (after Japan) - and may potentially exploit this leverage in its current trade war with the United States - by selling large quantities of treasuries in a short period of time, driving up bond yields.

Business Overview And 2024 Results By Operation

IAMGOLD Corporation is a Canadian based gold miner with three operating mines, including two in Canada and one in Burkina Faso.

Côté Gold (Canada)

The Côté Gold mine, based in Ontario, Canada, contributed 18.59% towards IAG's 2024 attributable gold production. See a virtual tour of the mine here. The mine started producing gold at the end of March 2024, and is currently ramping up to full capacity, which it expects to reach in Q4'2025. The average mined ore grade of 1.07 g/t in the last quarter of 2024 was in line with the mine plan. AISC's for Q4 were US$1,685 per ounce sold, and US$1,569 per ounce if excluding non-recurring costs related to construction activities at the tailings dam that will support the life-of-mine.

Côté ran into some operational challenges, which management described as typical for a new mine during the ramp-up phase and not a cause for concern.

In the Q4'2024 Report, management remarked that:

The target is to achieve the steady-state nameplate throughput rate of 36,000 tpd in the fourth quarter of 2025. In December, the plant operated at an average of 87% of the designed throughput level over a two-week period, prior to an unscheduled shutdown due to a split in a conveyor belt associated with ramp-up wear and tear. Repairs were made to the belts and replacement with a modified design was completed in January 2025. When online in December, the plant averaged 1,593 tph, or effectively 106% of nameplate, with a record daily throughput of 42,635 tonnes per day. Subsequent to quarter end, the HPGR rollers demonstrated accelerated wear necessitating a changeover ahead of schedule and limiting the secondary crushing capacity in January. The changeover of the HPGR rolls was completed in February 2025 with operating and maintenance procedures adjusted to maximize lifespan and optimize future changeover windows. Inside the plant, the grinding circuit was also impacted early in the quarter, due to repairs required on one of the Vertimills following a faulty start-up post-maintenance. Prevention and mitigation procedures have been put in place. Plant throughput is expected to be lower in the first quarter as a result of the impact of the timing of these maintenance issues, while operating guidance has been maintained.

2025 Production is expected to increase by between 81% and 101% at an AISC that is forecasted to be between 11% and 20% lower than that for the fourth quarter (which would make Côté the asset with the lowest AISC in the group).

Westwood Complex (Canada)

The Westwood Complex, located in Québec, Canada, saw exceptional operational performance as it delivered record annual gold production that was 3.07% above the top end of guidance, contributing 20.09% towards IAMGOLD's total attributable gold production. AISC's for Q4 were US$1,688.

2025 production is expected to be similar to 2024 - with management guiding that it will be within a range of 7% lower and 4% higher, and at an AISC that is forecasted to be between US$1,675/oz and US$1,825/oz (compared to US$1,702/oz for the 2024 financial year).

Essakane Mine (Burkina Faso)

IAMGOLD owns 90% of Essakane, with the Burkina Faso government owning the remaining 10% stake. Essakane delivered a 10% increase in attributable gold production for the full year, and contributed 61.32% towards IAMGOLD's total attributable production. 2024 AISC's were US$1,625/oz.

Management has guided that 2025 attributable production is expected to be between 2% and 12% lower, and that AISC's are expected to be in a range of between US$1,675/oz and US$1,825/oz.

In the Q4'2024 Report, management remarked that:

The cost guidance for 2025 is higher than the 2024 cost guidance ranges due to lower production and higher local spending including regional security expenditures, increased community programs, permit fees and taxes. A decrease in capitalized waste mining resulted in less waste stripping costs being capitalized and therefore in a higher proportion of costs included in cash costs with an equal reduction in capitalized waste stripping costs included in AISC.

IAMGOLD's 2025 Full-Year Guidance

Management has guided that 2025 consolidated production is expected to be between 33% and 44% higher, building on the 43% year-on-year growth achieved in 2024. AISC's in 2025 are forecasted to be within a range of US$1,625/oz to US$1,800/oz.

Risks

The most pertinent risk to IAMGOLD - aside from a substantial decline in the gold price - is related to political and security risk.

From the Q4'2024 Results:

The political and security environments in Burkina Faso and its neighbouring countries is distressed and volatility remains elevated, including in the Sahel region where the Company's Essakane mine is located. Burkina Faso and other neighbouring countries in the region experienced military coups in the past couple of years. Stability in the West African region, generally, has been upended by military coups and led to near-total suspension of military assistance from France and the U.S. and exclusion of military governments in joint military task force with other West African countries formed to prevent militant attacks and threats from further spreading in the rest of the region. Given Essakane's significant contribution to the financial condition of the Company, any terrorist attack, change in legislation or ownership, problematic or adverse condition affecting mining, processing, infrastructure, equipment, labour, the supply chain, taxation, legal or reputational status could have a material adverse effect on the Company's business, operations, liquidity and capital resources.

Considering that the operations in Burkina Faso contributed close to two-thirds of IAMGOLD's total attributable production in 2024, I am of the opinion that IAG should be trading at a lower Forward EV/EBITDA ratio than peers with a greater share of their operations in safer jurisdictions.

Valuation

I prefer using Forward EV/EBITDA ratios to value companies in the materials sector, as it guides me in understanding what I am paying for the underlying business versus the earnings that it is forecasted to generate.

In selecting the peer group for my analysis, I have chosen gold mining companies with a market cap larger than $1 billion, and excluded companies whose business models primarily rely on gold royalty and streaming contracts, as opposed to actually owning and operating physical gold mines.

Over the last five years, IAG has traded at an average FWD EV/EBITDA ratio that was 36.53% below that of its peers. IAG is now trading at a FWD EV/EBITDA ratio that is 36.12% below its peers - suggesting that it is fairly valued on a relative basis versus the peer group.

Would I Invest In IAG Now?

Although IAMGOLD delivered an impressive 61% return over the past year and has guided for a significant production increase in 2025 as operations at the Côté Gold mine ramp up, it is not my preferred gold miner.

Emerging market currencies, including the South African Rand, have come under pressure amid renewed risk-off sentiment following the recent market turmoil triggered by President Trump's "Reciprocal" tariffs. In addition, South Africa's newly formed Government of National Unity remains deadlocked over the national budget, adding further strain to the Rand (which has sold off 8.68% against the US Dollar over the last month).

For this reason, I currently see more value in gold mines that have a large proportion of their costs denominated in Rand, with stable production, while also operating at lower AISC's that provide some margin of safety should the gold price retreat.

Harmony Gold (NYSE:HMY), and Pan African Resources (OTCQX:PAFRY) in particular, look compelling to me at present.

data.title: March CPI: Cold Inflation Update Could Allow The Fed To Cut In May
data.description:
In the middle of an uncertain macro environment dominated by headlines about tariffs, the March CPI report provided a more optimistic view on the inflation situation. Soft monthly increases in headline and core inflation driven by declines in several key segments, including energy, may provide the Fed with enough room to cut rates in May if they so choose.

Headline Inflation

For the first time since the pandemic, U.S. CPI saw a monthly decline, coming in at -0.1% MoM in March. With the negative monthly print, the annual rate slowed to 2.4% YoY, the lowest since September 2024, and well below expectations of a slowdown to 2.6% YoY (from 2.8% YoY in February). The negative headline monthly rate was largely a result of energy prices which saw a strong decline in March.

  • The energy CPI component fell -2.4% MoM and -3.3% YoY, down from the -0.2% YoY increase in February. The monthly decline in energy was the largest since May 2023. The drop in gas prices in March drove the decline as that index fell -6.3% MoM (largest decline since December 2022). Its large weight offset rises in the natural gas index (+3.6% MoM) and electricity (+0.9% MoM).
  • Food prices continued their uptrend, posting a moderately strong increase of 0.4% MoM and pushing the annual rate up to 3.0% YoY which is the largest annual increase since October 2023. Food at home prices were up 0.5% MoM with meats up 1.1% MoM and dairy up 1.0% MoM. Food away from home increased 0.4% MoM with prices for full service meals up 0.6% MoM. The trend in food prices which is typically categorized as volatile remains in a steady uptrend. Tariffs are only going to accelerate the trend.

Core Inflation

While the decline in energy prices played a major role in the decline in the headline index, the core inflation segment did come in softer than expected at 0.1% MoM, a monthly rate not seen since May and June of 2024. That soft print helped push core CPI inflation below the three percent threshold to 2.8% YoY in March, the lowest in four years. The disinflation was a result of cool trends in both goods and services that pointed to a broad decline in prices.

  • The core goods segment declined -0.1% MoM in March, the first decline since August 2024. A major subsegment driving the decline was a -0.2% MoM move in transportation commodities that featured a -0.7% MoM decline in used vehicle prices. This offset a slight 0.1% MoM rise in new vehicle prices. There were declines in two other goods segments, medical goods (-1.1% MoM) and recreation goods (-0.3% MoM), and a flat MoM reading in household furnishings & supplies.
  • The core services segment was only up 0.1% MoM, the first increase of that size since August 2021. Another sizeable decline in transportation services played a major role in the soft core print. It fell -1.4% MoM in March, the largest MoM decline since May 2020, after an -0.8% MoM decline in February. Within that index, motor vehicle insurance prices fell -0.8% MoM (largest decline since October 2021) and airfares fell -5.3% MoM (largest decline since September 2021).

  • The shelter CPI component increased just 0.2% MoM, the slowest since June 2024, and the annual increase cooled to 4.0% YoY, the lowest since November 2021. The cool reading is a bit deceptive. The subindex was affected by a -3.5% MoM decline in lodging away from home, the largest drop in over three years, while the rent of primary index increased 0.3% MoM and owners’ equivalent rent was up 0.4% MoM.
  • If we remove the shelter and used car moves from the core CPI reading, prices were unchanged in the month of March for the first time since May 2024. On an annual basis, this special aggregate decelerated from 2.2% YoY in February to 1.8% YoY in March, the lowest in four years.

The soft March CPI print is evidence that weaker discretionary spending is flowing through into prices. Food and housing prices looked like the hottest segments this month while there were declines in discretionary segments like recreation goods and airfare and notable soft prints in recreation services (+0.1% MoM) and household furnishings (0.0% MoM). This is the kind of report that the Fed was looking for in its fight against inflation as it is evidence of demand-driven disinflation. Instead, over the last two years there was a steady strength in the economy that kept inflation sticky.

There is a major caveat to this report in that it is the last CPI reading before the major reciprocal tariffs went into effect and probably too early of a reading to see any impact from the tariffs placed on Mexican and Canadian imports. The impact from the North American tariffs was always going to be limited because most of these imports from the U.S.’s neighbors are for intermediate goods like energy, lumber, and machinery. Autos is the main exception here and new vehicle prices did increase 0.1% MoM, the largest so far in 2025.

Market Reaction

The market reaction to the soft CPI print was interesting. At first, there was a strong decline in Treasury rates as an immediate response to the report. The 2-year yield (US2Y) fell about -8 bps and the 10-year yield (US10Y) moved lower by about -4 bps. That move started to unwind about 10 minutes after the release. In fact, by about 8:50 am, the 10-year yield was back to where it was before the CPI report. Similarly, there was a sharp rise in S&P futures (SPX) when the report was released that was reversed in the next 15 minutes. In general, I think that the market recognizes that this is a dovish report, but the current market dynamics dominated by trade headlines have overwhelmed that sentiment.

What the market, especially the bond market, will want to see is how the Fed will shift its rhetoric in response to this report. The cool reading can provide FOMC members with an opportunity to become softer on inflation even with tariffs looming because it is evidence that prices were impacted by demand-driven factors at the end of the first quarter. Because of this, the Fed is also likely to see more downside risks to growth that are unrelated to tariffs, which could push against its narrative that the U.S. economy is clearly strong.

I believe that the Fed could use the soft March inflation report as a reason to cut rates in May. There is solid evidence of weaker demand-side pressure on prices, and this will also show in the next PCE price index update. Additionally, the Fed will be looking at volatility in the bond market which is significantly tightening credit conditions, and it will want to cut to help assuage some market anxiety.

Despite all of that, the Fed will have to balance this with how it sees tariffs impacting inflation even if there is a 90-day pause. The macroenvironment is still very uncertain, and almost impossible for the Fed to navigate flawlessly, but soft inflation in March is definitely good news for the monetary policymakers.

data.title: Investors Title Company: Exposure To The U.S. Housing Sector
data.description:
Introduction

Investors Title Company (NASDAQ:ITIC) is a property title insurance company, one of the lowest risk ventures within the insurance space. Losses and loss provisions on these contracts are very low, and the majority of the company’s expenses are related to paying the commissions to its sales agents.

The company’s financial results are very dependent on the transaction volumes on the real estate markets, and that’s why the dividend payments can fluctuate by quite a wide margin. ITIC’s share price has been strong in the past few years but recently lost some ground when the market started its correction, and as I still expect interest rates to decrease (which should increase the appetite for home buyers), I wanted to keep my fingers on the pulse here.

A profit increase thanks to higher premiums

Investors Title Company’s profit is closely correlated to the transaction volumes on the real estate markets. As the real estate markets gained momentum, ITIC reported a $204M revenue from written premiums, an increase of approximately 20% from the $171M in premiums it generated in 2023. All other revenue items remained relatively stable, which allowed ITIC to report a full-year revenue of $258M, an increase of almost $34M compared to the preceding year.

The operating expenses also increased, mainly on the back of a higher commission paid to agents, which increased by about $24M. That being said, the expenses related to ITIC’s own staff decreased by in excess of $4M, and this absorbed a portion of the impact from higher fees. The total amount of operating expenses increased by $20M, which means the pre-tax income jumped by approximately 50% to $39.5M.

After paying the relevant corporate taxes, the net profit for the year was $31.1M, resulting in an EPS of $16.43 based on the current share count of just under 1.9M shares outstanding.

At the end of last year, ITIC’s book value was just under $252M, with approximately $237M in tangible assets. Divided over the 1.886M shares that are currently outstanding, this results in a tangible book value of $125.5 per share. That’s an increase of approximately $1 per share compared to the $124.5 per share at the end of last year. That’s a pretty small increase, but that’s mainly because ITIC prefers to pay out the vast majority of its earnings in dividends in combination with a small share buyback of around $1M per year.

During 2024, the company paid just under $30M in dividends. The normal quarterly dividend is $0.46 per share, but the company aims to pay out a special dividend that fluctuates with the bottom-line result. In 2022 and 2023, the special dividends were just $3 and $4 per share respectively but during FY 2024, ITIC paid a special dividend of $14/share which brought the full-year dividend to $15.82 per share. This represents a dividend yield of just over 7% based on the current share price. But of course, it goes without saying these dividends will continue to fluctuate depending on ITIC’s financial performance and investors shouldn’t bank on getting fat dividend cheques every year.

Investment thesis

I currently have a small long position in Investors Title Company, but I haven’t added to that position. I was hoping the recent correction would have pushed the ITIC share price lower, but the stock has been quite resilient. I will keep an eye out for an opportunity to add to this position.

data.title: Alibaba: A TikTok Deal Casualty (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Investment Thesis

2025 has so far been a fabulous year for Alibaba (NYSE:BABA).

China’s leading e-commerce player finally pulled itself out of its multi-year bottom with a raft of updates on many AI fronts, launching LLMs that beat peers and notched top performances across most LLM benchmarks. I have been quite bullish on Alibaba through last year given its innovation efforts and the promising overhaul in the company’s management a couple of years ago.

I previously expected tariff headwinds to impose upon Alibaba and many other China stocks, as noted in a previous analysis on the company (see: Risks section), but believed that Chinese stocks were in a better place this time around.

Unfortunately, there seems to be a recurring theme that constantly appears to manifest during many press conferences and discussions regarding the current US administration’s strategy around tariffs — that China is unwilling to cooperate on negotiating a deal. More importantly, China is unwilling to negotiate specifically on a deal regarding TikTok, a deal that appears to be in the Trump 2.0 administration’s deepest interests.

And until I can gain some visibility on deal talk advancing around the TikTok deal, I believe Alibaba’s stock will see headwinds through the next quarter or two, leading me to downgrade Alibaba to a Hold.

How The TikTok Deal Holds Chinese Stocks Hostage

The Trump 2.0 sovereign/business interests in TikTok originated during the pandemic lockdowns in 2020, leading to the Trump 1.0 administration signing the deal to ban TikTok. The ban was eventually removed after a series of events involving the fallout of a late-2020 TikTok-takeover deal culminating in the Biden administration ordering a “broad review of potential security risks from Chinese tech companies.”

Fast-forward to 2024, the Biden administration passed a law in April last year that effectively forces either all Chinese stakeholders, including TikTok parent ByteDance, to divest from TikTok or face an all-out TikTok ban. Per the law, TikTok was given time until January 19 this year but got a last-minute 75-day reprieve after Trump 2.0’s EO. As I write this, TikTok has secured a second 75-day reprieve from Trump 2.0, implying compliance until June 9 of this year.

Unfortunately, the complicated nature of deals involving any kind of TikTok takeover also involves compliance with China’s rules on technology exports. In short, any potential TikTok deal will require approval from the Chinese government. This could possibly be a reason why TikTok’s Chinese stakeholders are slow-walking any efforts to sell/divest their stake in TikTok. This still leaves the current Trump 2.0 administration in a bind as to how to comply with the TikTok divestiture/sale law that was passed twelve months ago.

Most of Trump 2.0’s publicized motivation so far regarding the recent reciprocal country-wide tariff menu has been about rewiring America’s supply chains and onshoring manufacturing. Only Canada, China and potentially the EU moved to retaliate against Trump 2.0’s reciprocal tariff program.

Still, the Trump administration chose to suddenly pause the reciprocal tariffs this week, which is something I fully expected in some ways.

However, Trump singled out China in the recent pause on reciprocal tariffs, citing the country’s insistence on escalation of tariff-slapping by both countries. Remember, at the heart of this all, the TikTok sale/divestiture is still in limbo, threatened by China's constant insistence on securing its compliance for the deal to proceed. Trump recently reiterated that the TikTok deal is “on the table” despite China dragging its feet on the deal.

With no clear visibility about a TikTok deal yet, the increasing odds of a US/China showdown continue to keep rising with the US figuring out how to stay compliant with the TikTok divestiture/sale law. At the time of writing this post, US tariffs on China stand at 125%, while Chinese tariffs on the US stand at 84%.

There will be different bargaining chips thrown at the negotiating table from both sides, which will cause significant volatility while capping potential for expanding valuation premiums on Chinese stocks such as Alibaba. Yesterday, the US Treasury Secretary also hinted at the possibility of Chinese stocks getting delisted from the US. Although I do not believe the US will follow through with that just yet, it still will introduce volatility.

What does all of this have to do with Alibaba?

What this all does is momentarily throw cold water on the expanding valuation premiums of Chinese stocks, including Alibaba, which were getting a nice lift so far.

Alibaba’s Valuation Premium Expansion May Pause For A Bit

Therefore, until the US and China arrive at some form of resolution on the TikTok deal compliance, as well as the trade war between the two countries, I believe Alibaba’s valuation premium expansion will be on hold for a few months.

For now, Alibaba trades at a forward valuation of 11.7x FY25 earnings or 10.3x FY26 earnings. (FY25 ends in March every year.). While FY25 EPS growth is expected to grow 4.4%, FY26 EPS growth is expected to grow 13.5%, so these valuation premiums still look reasonable.

But since I feel China’s stock market PE, as noted in Exhibit B, will remain range-bound, I expect Alibaba as well to remain range-bound until the TikTok deal and US/China tariff turbulence settle. This leads me to downgrade Alibaba to a Hold rating due to falling confidence levels in the company sustaining any expansion in its valuation premium in the current trade climate.

Other Factors To Note

Over the long term, I still believe Alibaba has found itself a solid footing with the strong movements it has been making this year in AI. It started the year by making rapid-fire announcements, launching its Qwen family of LLMs that rivaled the performance of DeepSeek and OpenAI.

Most recently, the Chinese e-commerce giant has dropped its hat into the ‘AI-agent ring’ by launching multimodal models under the Qwen family of LLMs, targeting all its international customers as well.

This does look very promising for Alibaba, but unfortunately for the company, e-commerce and internal logistics is still a majority of its revenue, so the company’s growth in the latter two end markets can be threatened by the brewing uncertainty between the US and China.

If Alibaba’s resilient management can somehow demonstrate a path towards sustainable revenue growth and margin revisions to the upside despite the current trade climate, I will reevaluate my Hold rating.

Alternatively, if there does emerge some clarity in the TikTok deal, I believe the overall sentiment surrounding Alibaba and China stocks may once again look favorable.

Takeaway

At the moment, there does not appear to be any visible deal between the US and China on the sale/divestiture of TikTok. The US law requires compliance on TikTok either being sold or its Chinese investors divesting, while China argues that any such deal requires its approval, creating a deadlock for all China stocks, including Alibaba.

Plus, the unresolved trade disputes between the US and China remain unresolved, which may pressure Alibaba’s e-commerce and international logistics revenue segments.

Therefore, although I find Alibaba’s low-teen valuation premium reasonable, I am downgrading the stock to a Hold for now.

data.title: 5 Rocking Income Funds To Consider In This Market Correction
data.description:
When I first became interested in the stock and bond markets and was early on in my investing journey, I knew little about the details of my investments beyond what my financial advisor at the time explained to me. Most of that “knowledge” revolved around a set of mutual fund products that his investment firm promoted.
